paragraphs.text: “And Joseph was brought down to Egypt by the Midianites; and Potiphar, an officer of Pharaoh’s, captain of the guard, bought him” (Gen 39:1). And Potiphar became rich through Joseph, as Laban had been enriched through his father. Joseph’s mistress fell in love with him and said to him: “Lie with me” (Gen 39:7). But when, having tried every cunning, she could not move him, then, to overcome him, by a trick she brought him into her chamber. When she “caught him by his garment” and he, “leaving his garment in her hand, fled… and got him out” (Gen 39:12), then, fearing to become a laughing-stock in the eyes of her servants, she cried out with a loud voice, and the inhabitants of the house gathered, to become witnesses not of what she had wished to do, but of what she now intended to say.

Joseph might at this point have fled and returned to his father’s house; but flight was hateful to him who had been saved from adultery. And he resolved to endure, until he should see his dreams fulfilled. Joseph’s master came, heard the words of his wife, and the witnesses confirmed her words. He saw also Joseph’s garment, which plainly accused him. Therefore he cast him without his garment into prison, just as without his garment he had been cast into the pit in the wilderness.
